name: Persian yazatas
shortDescription: Yazatas are divine beings in Zoroastrianism, serving as intermediaries between Ahura Mazda and humanity. They embody virtues, concepts, and aspects of the natural world, acting as protectors and guides.
description: Yazatas (Avestan for 'worthy of worship' or 'divine beings') are a class of divinities in Zoroastrianism, serving as emanations or aspects of Ahura Mazda, the supreme god. They are not deities in the same sense as those in polytheistic religions, but rather represent divine powers, virtues, and principles. Yazatas act as intermediaries between Ahura Mazda and humanity, conveying blessings and assisting in the cosmic struggle against evil (Angra Mainyu). Each yazata is associated with specific aspects of creation, moral qualities, or natural phenomena. For example, Mithra embodies truth and covenants, Sraosha represents obedience and conscience, and Rashnu is the judge of the dead. The worship of yazatas involves prayers, offerings, and adherence to the moral principles they represent. Their roles are crucial in maintaining cosmic order (asha) and guiding souls towards salvation. The Chinvat Bridge, a bridge of judgment, plays a key role in the afterlife, where the actions of the deceased are weighed by yazatas like Rashnu and Mithra, determining their fate. The concept of yazatas reflects the Zoroastrian belief in a structured and moral universe, where divine beings actively participate in human affairs and the ongoing battle between good and evil. Adherence to the principles embodied by the yazatas is seen as essential for achieving spiritual progress and contributing to the ultimate triumph of Ahura Mazda.

sources.title: Zoroastrians: Their Religious Beliefs and Practices
sources.author: Mary Boyce

sources.title: The Oxford Handbook of Zoroastrianism
sources.author: Edited by Michael Stausberg and Yuhan Sohrab-Dinshaw Vevaina
